annotations.label: Monte Carlo methods

anchor.snippet: are a broad class of computational algorithms based on repeated random sampling

annotations.note: An informal description of a class of computational algorithms, not a mathematical statement formalizable in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Monte Carlo general pattern

anchor.snippet: tend to follow a particular pattern

annotations.note: A methodological pattern (define domain, sample, compute, aggregate), not a formal mathematical object.

annotations.label: Estimating π with a quadrant

anchor.snippet: consider a quadrant (circular sector) inscribed in a unit square

annotations.note: A worked numerical example; Mathlib has no formalization of this π-estimation procedure.

annotations.label: Integrals as expected values via law of large numbers

anchor.snippet: integrals described by the expected value of some random variable can be approximated by taking the empirical mean

annotations.note: The strong law of large numbers states the empirical mean `n⁻¹ • ∑ X i` converges a.e. to `μ[X 0]`, exactly the convergence underlying MC integration.

annotations.label: Markov chain Monte Carlo central idea

anchor.snippet: design a judicious Markov chain model with a prescribed stationary probability distribution

annotations.note: Markov kernel infrastructure exists, but Mathlib has no notion of a stationary distribution of a Markov chain nor the MCMC construction.

annotations.label: Ergodic theorem for MCMC stationary distribution

anchor.snippet: the stationary distribution is approximated by the empirical measures of the random states

annotations.note: Birkhoff-average ergodic infrastructure is present, but the pointwise ergodic theorem and convergence of empirical measures to a stationary distribution are not formalized.

annotations.label: Simple Monte Carlo method

anchor.snippet: The simple Monte Carlo method gives an estimate for

annotations.note: An algorithmic estimator description, not a formalized declaration in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Expected throws of three eight-sided dice

anchor.snippet: Suppose we want to know how many times we should expect to throw three eight-sided dice

annotations.note: A specific worked simulation example with no corresponding Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Estimated (sample) variance

anchor.snippet: be the estimated variance, sometimes called the

annotations.note: Mathlib defines the population variance `Var[X;μ]`, but not the sample/estimated variance computed from simulation outputs.

annotations.label: Sufficient sample size condition
annotations.note: A heuristic stopping/sample-size criterion for simulations, not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sample-size formula for bounded results

anchor.section: Formulae for simulations' results with bounds
anchor.snippet: An alternative formula can be used in the special case where all simulation results are bounded above and below

annotations.note: An applied sample-size formula for bounded outputs with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Sawilowsky's simulation / method / simulation distinction

anchor.snippet: distinguishes between a simulation , a Monte Carlo method, and a Monte Carlo simulation

annotations.note: Terminological distinctions, not mathematical content formalizable in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Characteristics of a high-quality Monte Carlo simulation

anchor.snippet: lists the characteristics of a high-quality Monte Carlo simulation

annotations.note: A list of qualitative desiderata, not a formal statement.

annotations.label: Quasi-Monte Carlo methods

anchor.snippet: Methods based on their use are called quasi-Monte Carlo methods

annotations.note: Low-discrepancy/quasi-Monte Carlo sequences are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Curse of dimensionality

anchor.snippet: This is called the curse of dimensionality

annotations.note: An informal phenomenon about exponential growth of cost with dimension, not a Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Central limit theorem convergence rate

anchor.snippet: quadrupling the number of sampled points halves the error, regardless of the number of dimensions

annotations.note: The 1-dimensional central limit theorem (with its `1/√n` scaling) is formalized, but the dimension-independent O(1/√N) error-rate statement is not stated as such.

annotations.label: Importance sampling

anchor.snippet: known as importance sampling in statistics, involves sampling the points randomly, but more frequently where the integrand is large

annotations.note: The importance sampling variance-reduction technique is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Traveling salesman optimization problem

anchor.snippet: In the traveling salesman problem the goal is to minimize distance traveled

annotations.note: The traveling salesman optimization problem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Probabilistic formulation of inverse problems

anchor.snippet: Probabilistic formulation of inverse problems leads to the definition of a probability distribution in the model space

annotations.note: The Bayesian/probabilistic formulation of inverse problems is not formalized in Mathlib.
